_cite.reason: URL-normalization 1c: strip trailing markdown-emphasis `*`/backtick glued to citation URLs in intelligence_item_sections.content_md (customer-checkable link + validate_item_provenance criterion-2 exact-compare). Operator ruling 2026-07-04. Same transform as the write-site fix.

prior.content_md:
Transport Canada's regulatory framework positions freight forwarders and cargo intermediaries primarily as **shippers/consignors** and, where applicable, as **agents acting on behalf of foreign air operators or domestic carriers**. The workspace does not operate aircraft and is therefore not directly subject to the Foreign Air Operator Certification Manual (TP 11524) as a certificate holder; however, it sits immediately upstream of FAOC holders on every air freight movement into or out of Canada. As the party preparing cargo documentation and tendering shipments to FAOC-holding carriers, the workspace is responsible for ensuring that all goods, including those carried in its live events, fine art, luxury goods, film and TV, high-value automotive, and humanitarian verticals, are correctly classified, declared, and packaged in conformity with the Canadian Aviation Regulations (CARs, SOR/96-433) and the Transportation of Dangerous Goods Regulations (TDG Regulations, SOR/2001-286, last amended 2024-10-25 and current to 2026-03-17).

Under the TDG Regulations, any entity that 'offers for transport' dangerous goods by air in Canada — a role the workspace may occupy when tendering pre-packed shipments — is classified as a **consignor** and bears statutory obligations for classification, documentation (shipping papers), marks, and labels. The workspace must confirm, with legal counsel, whether its operational model (e.g., consolidation, back-to-back AWBs, co-loading) causes it to be treated as a consignor or merely as an agent, because the compliance chain differs materially between those roles.

For marine movements, the workspace is similarly positioned as a shipper or NVOCC upstream of vessel operators subject to the Canada Shipping Act, 2001 and associated marine safety regulations. The proposed Canadian Marine Pilotage Regulations, 2025 and amendments to the Arctic Shipping Safety and Pollution Prevention Regulations are carrier-facing; the workspace's exposure is indirect but real — service disruptions, route-access changes, and port-security requirements flow downstream to cargo cut-off and documentation timelines.

The Aerodrome Certification Procedures (TP 7775) and Aircraft Ground Icing Operations Guidelines (TP 14052) govern ground-side airport operations and are directly relevant where the workspace holds a ground-handling or ramp-access role at Canadian airports. Legal confirmation is required on whether any warehouse, ramp, or cargo-handling functions performed by or contracted by the workspace at certified Canadian airports bring it within the scope of TP 7775 obligations.

*Source: Foreign Air Operator Certification Manual TP 11524 (2026 edition), Transport Canada Civil Aviation. https://tc.canada.ca/en/aviation/publications/foreign-air-operator-certification-manual-tp-11524. Transportation of Dangerous Goods Regulations SOR/2001-286, current to 2026-03-17, Justice Canada. https://laws-lois.justice.gc.ca/eng/regulations/SOR-2001-286/. Canadian Aviation Regulations SOR/96-433, current to 2026-03-31, Justice Canada. https://laws-lois.justice.gc.ca/eng/regulations/sor-96-433/.*
